questions.question: The defence of duress holds that a person may be excused from criminal liability where they committed the offence because of a threat of death or serious injury. What is the definitive Irish test for duress as articulated in DPP v Gleeson [2019] IESC?

options.A: The accused must prove that they subjectively believed the threat was genuine — no objective element is required; the test is purely subjective
options.B: The court applied a mixed subjective/objective test requiring: (1) there was a threat of death or serious injury; (2) the accused genuinely believed the threat was real and would be carried out; (3) a sober person of reasonable firmness sharing the accused's characteristics would have responded in the same way; and (4) there was no safe avenue of escape available to the accused
options.C: The test requires only that the threat caused the accused to act — if the threat was the operative cause of the offence, the defence is established regardless of the reasonableness of the accused's response
options.D: The defence requires that the accused reported the threat to the Gardaí before committing the offence — failure to report disqualifies the accused from relying on duress

questions.explanation: DPP v Gleeson [2019] IESC is the definitive Irish Supreme Court authority on the defence of duress. The court articulated a mixed subjective/objective test with four key elements: (1) the existence of a genuine threat of death or serious injury; (2) the accused must have genuinely (subjectively) believed the threat was real and would be carried out; (3) the threat must have been such that a sober person of reasonable firmness sharing the accused's characteristics would have responded in the same way — this is the objective element; and (4) there must have been no safe avenue of escape available to the accused — the accused must have been effectively trapped into committing the offence by the absence of any reasonable way out. This test balances sympathy for genuinely coerced accused persons with appropriate limits to prevent the defence from being too easily established. Option A incorrectly removes the objective element. Option C incorrectly removes the reasonableness requirement. Option D invents a pre-reporting requirement with no basis in law.

questions.question: Can duress be relied upon as a defence to a charge of murder in Irish law?

options.A: Yes — duress is a complete defence to all criminal charges, including murder, provided the four-part test is satisfied
options.B: No — the defence of duress is not available to a charge of murder in Irish law; the principle that a person cannot choose to kill an innocent person to save their own life (or another's) is established in AG v Whelan [1934] and the unavailability of the defence to murder is a firm rule of Irish criminal law
options.C: Yes — but only where the threat is of immediate death to the accused personally; threats to third parties cannot support a duress defence to murder
options.D: Yes — duress is available to murder charges but results in a reduction to manslaughter rather than a complete acquittal

questions.explanation: Duress is not available as a defence to murder in Irish law. This is one of the most important limitations on the defence of duress. The principle was established in AG v Whelan [1934] and has been consistently maintained: a person cannot justify killing an innocent victim by claiming they were threatened unless they complied. The rationale is that the law cannot allow a person to choose the death of an innocent victim to preserve their own life — the accused should be expected to sacrifice themselves rather than kill an innocent person. The House of Lords in R v Howe [1987] reached the same conclusion for English law. The unavailability of duress to murder reflects the unique gravity of intentional killing and is a non-negotiable limit on the defence. Note: duress may be available for other very serious offences (such as robbery, serious assault, and even some firearms offences) provided the Gleeson test is met. Option A incorrectly makes duress available to all charges. Options C and D incorrectly assume duress is available to murder in some circumstances.

questions.question: In R v Hasan [2005] HOL (England and Wales), the House of Lords considered the temporal requirements of duress. What did the court hold regarding the immediacy of the threat?

options.A: The court held that the threat need not be immediate — a future threat that the accused genuinely believes will be carried out is sufficient for duress
options.B: The court held that the threat must be sufficiently immediate and operating at the time the accused commits the offence — a past threat, a general background threat, or a threat of future harm that the accused could have taken steps to avoid does not satisfy the requirements of duress; the accused must be acting under the direct compulsion of an immediate threat
options.C: The court held that the immediacy of the threat is irrelevant — what matters is whether the accused genuinely believed at any point that the threat would be carried out
options.D: The court held that threats can operate over an extended period — a pattern of threats over months constitutes sufficient immediacy provided the accused acted consistently in response to each threat

questions.explanation: R v Hasan [2005] HOL significantly tightened the requirements for duress in English law, particularly regarding the immediacy of the threat. The House of Lords held that the threat must be sufficiently immediate and present at the time the offence is committed. Duress is not available where the threat is of future harm that the accused could have avoided by taking reasonable steps (such as going to the police, escaping, or removing themselves from the situation). The Hasan decision was a deliberate restriction of the defence — the Lords expressed concern that a too-generous approach to duress was being exploited. Hasan is relevant to Irish law as a persuasive authority, consistent with the Gleeson requirement that there be no safe avenue of escape. Option A incorrectly accepts future threats as sufficient. Option C removes the immediacy requirement entirely. Option D incorrectly treats a pattern of threats over months as satisfying immediacy.

questions.question: In R v Hudson and Taylor [1971] CA (England and Wales), the accused women failed to give truthful evidence at trial, claiming they had been threatened with violence if they testified against a gang. Their fear was of future, not immediate, harm. The Court of Appeal allowed their duress defence. How was this case subsequently treated?

options.A: Hudson and Taylor was confirmed and expanded — it established that any credible threat, even of future harm, is sufficient for duress regardless of when it would be carried out
options.B: Hudson and Taylor was significantly limited by R v Hasan [2005] HOL — the House of Lords expressed doubt about the correctness of allowing duress on the basis of a threat that was not sufficiently immediate; the trend of English law has moved toward a stricter immediacy requirement than Hudson and Taylor suggested
options.C: Hudson and Taylor was overruled and is no longer good law in England and Wales or Ireland
options.D: Hudson and Taylor was confirmed in DPP v Gleeson [2019] IESC as the definitive authority on the temporal requirements of duress in Ireland

questions.explanation: R v Hudson and Taylor [1971] CA allowed a duress defence even where the threatened harm was not immediately imminent at the moment of the offence — the women were afraid of what would happen if they gave evidence against the gang, and the Court of Appeal held the threat sufficiently operative. However, R v Hasan [2005] HOL cast significant doubt on the approach in Hudson and Taylor, holding that the threat must be more immediately pressing. The Lords in Hasan considered the Hudson and Taylor approach too generous and expressed the view that threats of future harm that the accused could have taken steps to avoid should not support duress. Hudson and Taylor has not been formally overruled in England and Wales but its authority has been substantially undermined by Hasan. In Irish law, the Gleeson test (requiring no safe avenue of escape) is more consistent with the Hasan approach. Option A mischaracterises Hudson and Taylor's current standing. Option C overstates — it has not been formally overruled. Option D incorrectly states Gleeson confirmed Hudson and Taylor.

questions.question: In R v Martin [1989] CA (England and Wales), the court considered whether the accused's personal characteristics are relevant to the objective element of the duress test. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that the objective element is assessed by reference to a completely abstract reasonable person with no specific characteristics — the accused's personal circumstances are irrelevant
options.B: The court held that the objective test ('reasonable person of firm character') must be applied to a person sharing the relevant characteristics of the accused — relevant personal characteristics (such as age, pregnancy, physical disability) may be taken into account when assessing whether a sober person of reasonable firmness would have responded in the same way as the accused
options.C: The court held that the accused's characteristics are relevant only to the subjective element — once the accused genuinely believed the threat, the objective element is not applied
options.D: The court held that the objective element of duress is satisfied wherever the accused commits a less serious offence in response to a threat of a more serious harm — proportionality is the only objective standard

questions.explanation: R v Martin [1989] CA addressed the important question of what characteristics the reasonable person in the objective test for duress shares with the accused. The court held that the objective element — would a sober person of reasonable firmness have responded in the same way — must be applied to a person sharing the relevant characteristics of the accused. This is important: relevant characteristics (age, pregnancy, serious physical disability) can be taken into account when assessing the standard expected. However, not all characteristics are relevant — characteristics that go to firmness of mind (such as vulnerability to pressure generally, or a tendency to panic) would not typically be attributed to the 'reasonable firm person.' The Martin approach is incorporated into the Gleeson test in Irish law. Option A incorrectly makes the objective element completely abstract. Option C incorrectly removes the objective element once subjective belief is established. Option D incorrectly replaces the objective test with a proportionality test.

questions.question: In R v Fitzpatrick [1977] NICA (Northern Ireland), the accused joined a paramilitary organisation and was subsequently threatened into committing offences on behalf of the organisation. The court considered whether duress was available where the accused had voluntarily joined the group that later threatened him. What did the court hold?

options.A: The court held that voluntary membership of the group was irrelevant — the defence of duress focuses only on the circumstances at the time of the offence, not on how the accused came to be in that situation
options.B: The court held that where a person voluntarily joins an organisation knowing that it is the type of organisation that uses violence to compel members to commit offences, they cannot rely on the defence of duress when they are subsequently threatened by that organisation — the accused's voluntary entry into the situation that created the duress disqualifies them from the defence
options.C: The court held that voluntary membership of a criminal organisation reduces but does not eliminate the duress defence — it goes to mitigation rather than to availability of the defence
options.D: The court held that duress is always available to members of criminal organisations who are compelled to commit offences — the threat overrides the initial voluntary association

questions.explanation: R v Fitzpatrick [1977] NICA established the important principle that the defence of duress is not available where the accused's own voluntary actions created the situation in which they were later threatened — so-called 'self-induced duress.' Where a person voluntarily joins a criminal or violent organisation, knowing that it operates by compelling its members through threats and violence, they cannot claim duress when they are subsequently threatened into committing offences. The rationale is that the accused brought the duress upon themselves through their own free choice. This principle has been applied and confirmed in R v Hasan [2005] HOL and is consistent with the general principle that a defence is not available where the accused was at fault in creating the circumstances giving rise to it. Option A incorrectly ignores how the accused came to be in the situation. Option C incorrectly treats self-induced duress as mitigation rather than barring the defence. Option D incorrectly makes duress always available.

questions.question: In DPP v Paul Carew (Ireland), the court considered whether duress was available where the accused had not taken advantage of a safe avenue of escape. What principle did the case confirm?

options.A: The court held that the availability of a safe avenue of escape is irrelevant — if the accused genuinely believed the threat would be carried out, the defence stands even if they failed to escape when they could have
options.B: The court confirmed that where there was a safe avenue of escape available to the accused which they failed to take, the defence of duress is unavailable — the accused must have been genuinely trapped by the threat with no reasonable means of avoiding both the threat and the offence
options.C: The court held that failure to escape when possible reduces the duress defence to mitigation but does not eliminate it — it is a factor that goes to the weight of the evidence of duress
options.D: The court held that the accused is only required to attempt escape where the safe avenue of escape is obvious and immediate — theoretical possibilities of escape do not disqualify the accused from the defence

questions.explanation: DPP v Paul Carew confirmed the Irish position that the defence of duress is unavailable where the accused had a safe avenue of escape and failed to take it. This is consistent with the fourth element of the Gleeson test — the accused must have had no safe avenue of escape available. The rationale is that duress excuses only where the accused genuinely had no choice: if the accused could have avoided both the threat and the need to commit the offence by taking a reasonable step (such as going to the Gardaí, fleeing the situation, or otherwise removing themselves), then they did have a choice and duress should not excuse their conduct. The Carew principle prevents the defence from being used where the accused, though threatened, could have avoided the outcome. The assessment of what constitutes a safe avenue of escape is fact-sensitive and takes into account the accused's circumstances. Option A incorrectly removes the escape requirement. Option C treats failure to escape as merely mitigating. Option D introduces a limitation on the escape requirement that is not part of the law.

questions.question: Alan is told by a gang member: 'If you don't drive the getaway car for our bank robbery tomorrow, we will kill your wife.' Alan, terrified, drives the car. He is later charged with robbery as a secondary party. Applying the Irish law on duress (DPP v Gleeson), advise on the availability of the defence.

options.A: Duress is clearly available — Alan was threatened with the death of his wife and genuinely feared the threat would be carried out; the test is easily satisfied
options.B: Duress may be available, subject to detailed factual analysis: (1) the threat of death is serious; (2) Alan must have genuinely believed it would be carried out; (3) a reasonable person of firm character sharing Alan's characteristics must have responded in the same way; (4) there must have been no safe avenue of escape (e.g., going to the Gardaí, removing his wife to safety); if all four elements are satisfied, duress is a complete defence to the robbery charge; the non-immediacy of the threat (the robbery is 'tomorrow') may be scrutinised under the Hasan immediacy requirement
options.C: Duress is not available — threats to third parties (Alan's wife) rather than to Alan personally cannot support the duress defence
options.D: Duress is not available — Alan is charged as a secondary party and duress can only be raised by principal offenders, not accessories

questions.explanation: This scenario requires careful application of the Gleeson test to a realistic fact pattern. The four elements must each be examined: (1) Threat of death — the threat to kill Alan's wife is a qualifying threat (death or serious injury); threats to family members can qualify, not just threats to the accused personally; (2) Genuine belief — Alan must genuinely have believed the threat would be carried out; (3) Objective reasonableness — a reasonable person of firm character sharing Alan's circumstances must have responded the same way; (4) No safe avenue of escape — the critical issue here; was there an opportunity to go to the Gardaí, remove his wife, or otherwise avoid the situation? Additionally, the threat being of future (tomorrow's) harm, not immediate harm, raises the Hasan immediacy point. The defence is potentially available but is by no means automatic — the facts require rigorous analysis. Option A is too confident — the defence requires careful analysis, particularly regarding escape avenues and immediacy. Option C incorrectly limits duress to threats to the accused personally — threats to family members qualify. Option D incorrectly limits duress to principal offenders.

questions.question: The Law Reform Commission (Ireland) in its 2006 Report considered codifying the defence of duress. What approach did it recommend?

options.A: The LRC recommended abolishing the defence of duress entirely — it concluded that duress was too easily abused and should not excuse serious criminal conduct
options.B: The LRC recommended codifying the duress defence in statute with a clear statement of its elements — this would include the mixed subjective/objective test, the requirement that there be no safe avenue of escape, the exclusion of self-induced duress, and the unavailability of the defence to murder; however, this recommendation has not been enacted in statute and duress remains a common law defence in Irish law
options.C: The LRC recommended extending duress to murder charges where the threat is of the accused's own imminent death — the commission concluded the existing exclusion was too harsh
options.D: The LRC recommended replacing duress with a statutory 'necessity' defence that would be available to all offences including murder

questions.explanation: The Law Reform Commission's 2006 Report on Defences in Criminal Law recommended the codification of the defence of duress in a manner that would give statutory form to the existing common law elements. The LRC's recommended codification would preserve the mixed subjective/objective test, the absence of safe avenue of escape requirement, the exclusion where duress is self-induced, and the unavailability of duress as a defence to murder. The Report's recommendations have not been enacted — duress remains a common law defence in Irish law, now with authoritative guidance from DPP v Gleeson [2019] IESC. FE-1 candidates should be aware that the LRC recommended codification but that it has not occurred. Option A incorrectly states the LRC recommended abolition. Option C incorrectly states the LRC recommended extending duress to murder — the LRC maintained the exclusion for murder. Option D incorrectly describes the LRC's recommendation as replacing duress with a necessity defence.
